title: Universal Topological Power Transfer with Arbitrarily Large Chern Number in Driven Quantum Spin Chains
authors: Anshuman Tripathi, Mircea Trif, Thore Posske
abstract: Topological frequency converters exploit a quantized transfer of power between two driving fields in a quantum system, a phenomenon topologically protected by the Chern number of the associated fiber bundle. While realizations with few-spin systems have theoretically demonstrated this effect, the conversion factors have typically been restricted to small integer values. Here, we investigate an interacting $XXZ$ Heisenberg spin-$1/2$ chain driven adiabatically by two magnetic drives with incommensurate frequencies. The Chern number, determined by the degeneracy points enclosed by the adiabatic trajectory, increases systematically with the chain length and can be tuned through the exchange anisotropy, providing direct control of the topological pumping strength. We reveal a universal dependency of the anisotropy and magnetic field strength for odd and even chain lengths of the quantum critical points. This provides a mechanism for a topological frequency converter with an arbitrarily large, quantized conversion ratio in interacting quantum spin chains. The mechanism remains the same for arbitrary coupling regions of the drives.
summary: This paper presents a theoretical mechanism for achieving arbitrarily large, quantized power transfer between two driving frequencies. By using an interacting XXZ Heisenberg spin chain, the authors show that the Chern number—which dictates the conversion ratio—increases linearly with the system size. This allows for a topological frequency converter with a tunable and potentially massive amplification factor. The study also identifies universal scaling laws for the quantum critical points that govern this process.
main_problem: Existing topological frequency converters are limited to small integer Chern numbers, which restricts the possible amplification and conversion factors of power transfer between driving fields.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that in an interacting XXZ Heisenberg spin chain, the Chern number scales linearly with the chain length N, enabling a topological frequency converter with an arbitrarily large, quantized conversion ratio.
method: The study uses the Lanczos algorithm for ground state calculation, the Crank-Nicolson method for time evolution, and a gauge-invariant projection method to calculate the Chern number via Berry flux summation.
model_or_system: An interacting XXZ Heisenberg spin-1/2 chain of length N, where the first spin is coupled to a static magnetic field and the last spin is driven by two incommensurate, circularly polarized magnetic drives.
key_observables: Time-averaged pumped power (P), Chern number (C), ground-state fidelity, and energy transfer.
important_parameters: Exchange anisotropy (delta/J), magnetic field strength (B), chain length (N), and driving frequencies (omega1, omega2).
main_assumptions: The system operates in the adiabatic or quasi-adiabatic regime, and the driving frequencies are sufficiently small to maintain the system near the instantaneous ground state.
figures_summary: Figure 3 compares energy transfer and fidelity in adiabatic vs. diabatic regimes; Figure 4 shows a general frequency converter setup; Figure 5 illustrates the scaling of hyperbolic fit parameters (b and c) with chain length N.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of limited conversion factors, describes the XXZ spin chain model and driving mechanism, presents numerical results for Chern number scaling and degeneracy trajectories, analyzes the scaling of critical points using hyperbolic fits, and concludes with discussion on experimental platforms and limitations.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and quantum optics as it provides a scalable mechanism for high-gain, quantized energy/frequency conversion using topological protection in interacting systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The frequency converter setup. (a) The XXZ spin-1/2 chain, where the first spin is coupled to a static magnetic reservoir pointing in x-direction, and the last spin is coupled to the two incommensurate circularly polarized magnetic drives in the xz (blue field) and xy (red field) planes. (b) The self-intersecting torus traced by the drives for field strength B = 1/2. The black dots mark two degeneracy points, the one enclosed by the torus yields the Chern number C = 1 for a chain length N = 8 and anisotropy ∆= −0.78J.

FIG. 2. The topological sectors, separated by quantum critical points, i.e., parameters of gap closure δE = 0. The universal collapse of trajectories as a function of anisotropy ∆and the magnetic field B pointing in the −x direction. The respective markers are the numerical value of the degeneracy calculated for (a) even and (b) odd chain lengths, and the colored curves are the hyperbolic fits (∆/J −cos(π/N))(B + b) = c, see Appendix B for values. The critical points accumulate in dependence on the chain length N for both the odd and even cases. By choosing the magnitude and starting point of B, diverse topological pumps can be realized.

FIG. 3. Dynamics of energy transfer (a) and the corresponding ground-state fidelity (b). Time evolution is simulated for a spin chain of length N = 4 with parameters ∆= J and B = 1, driven by incommensurate frequencies related by the golden ratio, ω2 = ω1(1 + √

FIG. 4. A general frequency converter setup with distributed boundary driving and fine tuning to the degeneracy point. A wide variety of driving protocols can be engineered by apply- ing different combinations of static tuning fields to arbitrary sites to establish the degeneracy landscape, while subjecting other localized spins to the incommensurate phase drives.

FIG. 5. Scaling of the hyperbolic fit parameters with chain length N. (a) The parameter c of the new accumulated trajectories with increase of N, plotted on a logarithmic scale. (b) The parameter b (for N ≥3 as b = 0 for N = 2), plotted on a logarithmic scale.
